name: Kigali Genocide Memorial (Gisozi)
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: The principal national memorial and final resting place for over 250,000 victims. Three permanent exhibitions document the genocide, a children's memorial, and a comparative exhibition on global genocidal violence.

testimony.text: We hid for weeks. When it was finally over and we emerged, the streets were silent. Everyone was gone. I walked through neighborhoods I had known my whole life and recognized nothing. The memorial gives them a place — a name, a grave, dignity. Without it, the world would forget them. I will not let them be forgotten.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor testimony, Kigali Genocide Memorial archives

context: Opened on the tenth anniversary of the genocide in April 2004, the memorial was established through a partnership between the Kigali City Council and the Aegis Trust. Remains were brought from across the capital — from streets, rivers, and shallow graves — and interred in mass graves in lots of 100,000. The memorial includes a Wall of Names that continues to grow as victims are identified. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2023.

name: Murambi Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A former technical school where an estimated 45,000–65,000 Tutsi were directed by authorities under false promises of French military protection. Water and food were cut off before the attack. Preserved mummified remains bear witness to the scale of the atrocity.

testimony.text: They told us the French soldiers would protect us at the school. Thousands walked for hours to reach it. When we arrived, there was no water. No food. We grew weaker every day. Then on the night of the twenty-first, they came. I survived because I was buried under others. I carry them with me in every breath.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Murambi memorial records

context: The Murambi Technical School was still under construction in April 1994 when local officials directed Tutsi civilians there, claiming French troops would provide protection. After days without provisions, soldiers and Interahamwe militia attacked on the night of April 21. The memorial, founded in 1995, displays preserved remains in the former classrooms. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

name: Bisesero Genocide Memorial
type: Memorial of Resistance & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: Known as the "Memorial of Resistance," honoring the Tutsi community of Bisesero who organized armed defense of the hills for over two months before being overwhelmed. Of an original community exceeding 50,000, approximately 1,500 survived.

testimony.text: We fought with stones, with sticks, with anything we could find. Every day they came, and every day we fought. For sixty-two days we held the hills. When the French soldiers finally arrived, they said they would return to help. They came back three days later. In those three days, thousands more of us died. We were the Hill of Resistance. We are still standing.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts of Bisesero resistance, multiple survivor testimonies

context: Bisesero's hills saw the longest sustained resistance of the genocide. The Tutsi community, with a historical tradition of self-defense, organized themselves against militia and military forces from April through late June. French forces from Operation Turquoise made initial contact on June 27 but delayed their return, during which thousands more were killed. The memorial has nine buildings representing the nine former communes of Kibuye Province. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

name: Nyamata Genocide Memorial
type: National Memorial & UNESCO World Heritage Site
desc: A Catholic church in Bugesera district, 35 km from Kigali, where over 10,000 people seeking sanctuary were killed. The church preserves bloodstained clothing, personal belongings, and human remains. The iron door still bears machete scars.

testimony.text: I was a child when we ran to the church. My mother told me that God would protect us in His house. When they broke through the iron door, I hid under the bodies. I could hear everything. I lay there for two days before I moved. The clothes hanging in that church — those were worn by people I knew. By my family. This church is their voice now.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented child survivor testimony, Nyamata memorial records

context: The Nyamata Catholic Church served as a traditional place of refuge for the Tutsi community of Bugesera, which had experienced periodic persecution since 1959. When the genocide began, thousands gathered at the church. On April 14–15, 1994, militia and soldiers broke through the reinforced iron doors and massacred those inside and in the surrounding grounds. The memorial, preserving the church as it was found, was established in 1997. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

name: Ntarama Genocide Memorial
desc: A former Catholic church 1 km from Nyamata, preserved exactly as it was found. The floor contains bones intermingled with clothing, shoes, and personal items. Approximately 5,000 people were killed here.

testimony.text: The church at Ntarama was small, but we believed we would be safe inside. They came with grenades first, then machetes. When you walk in today, you walk on the floor where we fell. The bones you see were people who sang hymns on Sunday. People who shared food with their neighbors. Do not look away. They deserve to be seen.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor accounts, Ntarama memorial records

context: Ntarama Church, located in Bugesera district just 1 km from Nyamata, was one of the first genocide memorials established in Rwanda in 1995, with the support of the genocide survivors' organization Ibuka. The memorial preserves the church interior as it was found after the massacre, including human remains, clothing, and personal effects.

name: Nyarubuye Genocide Memorial
desc: A former Catholic church, convent, and school of the Benebikira Sisters. Approximately 20,000 people were killed at this site, making it one of the largest individual massacre locations.

testimony.text: Nyarubuye was supposed to be different. The parish had always been a safe place. When the killing started, people came from every direction — from the hills, from the valleys. By the time the journalists arrived weeks later and showed the world what happened here, there was no one left to tell them. I tell it now because I am one of the few who can.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented witness accounts, Nyarubuye memorial archives

context: Nyarubuye parish in the Eastern Province became one of the first massacre sites documented by international media, when journalists arrived weeks after the killings. The compound held an estimated 20,000 people who had gathered from the surrounding hills. The attack began on April 15 and continued for several days.

name: Nyange Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Western Province, a site where Tutsi sought refuge before being attacked. The memorial honors those who perished and serves as a place for community remembrance.

testimony.text: At Nyange, we had no weapons. We had only each other and our faith. When they demolished the walls onto us, we held hands. I felt the weight of the building and the weight of every person I had ever known. Some of us were pulled out. Most were not.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, CNLG memorial records

context: The Nyange memorial site commemorates one of many massacres in the Western Province where Tutsi civilians gathered at churches and public buildings.

name: Kibuye Genocide Memorial
desc: Near Lake Kivu, honoring the estimated 12,000 victims killed at Gatwaro Stadium and surrounding sites in Kibuye prefecture.

testimony.text: Kibuye was beautiful. The lake, the hills, the churches. They used our trust in these places against us. When the prefect told us to gather at the stadium for our own safety, we believed him. We had always believed the authorities. That belief killed us.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented survivor testimony, Kibuye region

context: Kibuye prefecture in the Western Province experienced among the highest proportional death tolls during the genocide. Local authorities directed Tutsi civilians to gather at Gatwaro Stadium and churches, claiming it was for their protection.

name: Rebero Genocide Memorial
desc: Located on Rebero hill in Kigali, this memorial commemorates both Tutsi civilians and political figures who were targeted or who bravely opposed the genocide.

testimony.text: On Rebero hill, they killed politicians who refused to participate. They killed leaders who said no. These were people with the power to run, to hide, to leave the country. They stayed. They said no. And they were killed for it. We remember their courage here.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented historical records, Rebero memorial

context: The Rebero memorial uniquely honors both civilian victims and political figures who resisted the genocide. An estimated 14,400 people are interred at the site.

name: Nyanza Genocide Memorial (Kicukiro)
desc: Located at the former ETO school in Kicukiro district. When UN peacekeepers withdrew on April 11, thousands of civilians were marched to Nyanza hill and massacred.

testimony.text: The soldiers — the blue helmets — they were our only hope. When they started packing their vehicles, a silence fell over all of us. Mothers stopped singing to their children. We watched them drive away. Then we were marched to the hill. On April 11th every year, I stand on that hill and I remember what it means to be abandoned by the world.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts of the ETO/Kicukiro withdrawal

context: The Nyanza memorial at the ETO (École Technique Officielle) in Kicukiro commemorates one of the most documented failures of international intervention. Belgian UNAMIR peacekeepers were ordered to withdraw on April 11, abandoning approximately 4,000 civilians.

name: Mugombwa Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Southern Province, commemorating victims from the Gisagara district area.

testimony.text: In Mugombwa, the hills that had fed us became the hills that hid us. But they searched every hill. Every valley. Every home. The memorial stands where we could not.
testimony.attribution: Based on community memorial records, Southern Province

context: The Mugombwa memorial commemorates victims from the Gisagara district, part of the former Butare prefecture. Killings intensified after the replacement of the moderate Tutsi prefect in mid-April 1994.

name: Bigogwe Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Nyabihu district near the Virunga mountains, this memorial honors victims from the highlands region.

testimony.text: We lived in the shadow of the volcanoes. The mountains that had always protected us could not protect us from our neighbors. At Bigogwe, we remember that geography cannot save you from ideology.
testimony.attribution: Based on community records, Bigogwe area

context: Bigogwe, in the highlands near the Virunga volcanoes, was a site where Tutsi communities in the remote mountainous region were systematically targeted.

name: Busogo Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Northern Province near Musanze, commemorating victims from the northern highlands.

testimony.text: In the north, near the mountains, people thought the killing would not reach us. It reached us. It reached everywhere.
testimony.attribution: Based on community memorial records, Northern Province

context: The Busogo memorial commemorates victims from the northern highlands near Musanze district.

name: Butare University Memorial
desc: At the National University of Rwanda in Butare, commemorating students, faculty, and intellectuals who were targeted.

testimony.text: The university was where we came to build Rwanda's future. Tutsi professors, Tutsi students — we studied together, we ate together. Then they were told to separate us. Our classmates pointed us out. Knowledge could not save us. But remembering them honors what they were building.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts from the National University of Rwanda

context: The National University of Rwanda in Butare was the country's premier academic institution. When the genocide reached Butare after the prefect's replacement on April 19, Tutsi faculty and students were specifically targeted.

name: Muyumbu Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Rwamagana district, commemorating victims killed along the main eastern road corridor from Kigali.

testimony.text: Muyumbu was on the road east from Kigali. The roadblocks caught everyone trying to flee the capital. There was nowhere to run that did not have a checkpoint, a barrier, a list.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, Eastern Province records

context: Muyumbu, along the main road east from Kigali toward Rwamagana, was a site where roadblocks intercepted Tutsi civilians fleeing the capital.

name: Mutete Genocide Memorial
desc: Located in the Western Province hills, commemorating victims from the Karongi district area.

testimony.text: In the hills of Mutete, people tried to organize resistance like in Bisesero. But we were fewer. We had less time. The memorial marks where ordinary people tried to become heroes and were cut down.
testimony.attribution: Based on documented accounts, Western Province records

context: The Mutete memorial commemorates victims from the Karongi district area. Killings were coordinated with the broader Kibuye prefecture massacres.
